This isn't something that happened overnight; it took generations for these professions to become open to everyone who wanted them.\n\nFrom my perspective, growing up with a father holding a bachelor's degree but myself only having upper secondary education because I preferred spending time outdoors playing football or running rather than staying in classrooms - well, carpentry school was different though. response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
The gender gap in science, as I understand it from reading and my own experience working with measurements on-site, is similar to what I see when trying to square circles or balance beams. It's about numbers not being equal where men are often more represented than women in technical fields like physics and engineering. This isn't something that happened overnight; it took generations for these professions to become open to everyone who wanted them.

From my perspective, growing up with a father holding a bachelor's degree but myself only having upper secondary education because I preferred spending time outdoors playing football or running rather than staying in classrooms - well, carpentry school was different though. It taught me practical things which are useful too. But seeing the imbalance now reminds me of how even tasks that require precision can be done differently by everyone.

I think yes we should address it because fairness isn't just about getting numbers right but ensuring opportunities exist for all regardless of their gender. My own life is proof - married with two kids, still working full-time as a carpenter despite moderate extraversion and agreeableness scores don't help much in this office job scenario you're describing, I have to keep it real. The way we go about fixing this requires understanding why women might feel less inclined towards certain subjects without overstepping any boundaries related to conscientiousness or openness that vary across individuals.

In terms of how, perhaps more educational support and flexible work environments are needed? Like what my father taught me - a bit of practical help sometimes makes all the difference.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
The user's question is about addressing gender disparities in STEM fields. From analyzing the topic, it involves understanding structural inequalities that have persisted historically. Considering personal traits such as creativity and moderate extraversion but low openness scores shapes an approach grounded in tangible evidence rather than abstract theories.

The response should be accessible given limited formal education background yet still reflect deep-seated issues observed over a lifetime of experience. Drawing parallels from one's own profession emphasizes practical solutions that address real-world obstacles without alienating any group.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic and grounded, with occasional touches of frustration derived from lived experiences
